title: Anomalous tunneling as a low-energy theorem for Nambu-Goldstone modes
authors: Keisuke Fujii, Daichi Kagamihara, Masaru Hongo
abstract: Anomalous tunneling refers to the phenomenon in which the transmission coefficient through a potential barrier approaches unity as the energy of an incident particle or quasiparticle tends to zero. This counterintuitive effect has been reported in systems exhibiting spontaneous symmetry breaking (SSB), such as superfluids, yet the general conditions for its occurrence remain unclear. In this Letter, we establish that anomalous tunneling of Nambu-Goldstone (NG) modes is a universal low-energy theorem dictated solely by symmetry and scaling, using a low-energy effective field theory (EFT) framework. We formulate the scattering of NG modes by external potentials in terms of spatially dependent EFT coefficients and demonstrate that symmetry-preserving localized potentials are irrelevant in the long-wavelength limit, leading to perfect transmission. In contrast, symmetry-breaking perturbations are relevant and suppress transmission, resulting in the absence of anomalous tunneling. We illustrate this universal behavior with explicit examples of superfluid phonons and magnons.
summary: This paper establishes that the phenomenon of anomalous tunneling in broken-symmetry systems is a universal consequence of symmetry and scaling. It demonstrates that while symmetry-preserving defects become invisible at low energies, symmetry-breaking defects effectively block transmission. This result provides a unified theoretical rule for the behavior of Nambu-Goldstone modes like phonons and magnons.
main_problem: The paper seeks to establish the general conditions under which 'anomalous tunneling'—where the transmission coefficient approaches unity as energy tends to zero—occurs in systems with spontaneous symmetry breaking.
main_result: The authors prove that anomalous tunneling is a universal low-energy theorem where symmetry-preserving potentials are irrelevant and lead to perfect transmission, whereas symmetry-breaking perturbations are relevant and suppress transmission.
method: The study uses a low-energy effective field theory (EFT) framework combined with scaling analysis and dimensional power counting to evaluate the impact of localized potentials.
model_or_system: The framework applies to Nambu-Goldstone modes (Type-A and Type-B) in systems with spontaneous symmetry breaking, specifically illustrating the effect with superfluid phonons and magnons in ferromagnets.
key_observables: Transmission coefficient (T), reflection coefficient (R), and energy flux (Q).
important_parameters: Low-energy/low-momentum limit (k -> 0), symmetry-preserving vs. symmetry-breaking perturbations, and the scaling dimension of localized potentials.
main_assumptions: The analysis for Type-B modes neglects subleading omega^2 g_t(x) terms to focus on the low-energy limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the scaling analysis showing how potentials shrink or grow under scaling; Figure 2 shows the transmission coefficient approaching 1 as the momentum-width product (kia) approaches 0.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of anomalous tunneling, establishes a general EFT framework for Nambu-Goldstone modes, performs scaling and power-counting analysis to distinguish perturbation types, and provides explicit examples using phonons and magnons.
why_interesting: It provides a universal, symmetry-based principle for transport phenomena in many-body systems, which is highly relevant for understanding how excitations move through defects in BECs and other quantum fluids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of our result. At low en- ergies, the problem reduces to scattering of the NG mode in an effective potential Veff(x). Under scaling, symmetry- preserving potentials shrink, leading to perfect transmission, whereas symmetry-breaking ones grow, leading to perfect re- flection.
FIG. 2. Transmission coefficient T in Eq. (24) as a function of kia for several values of γ. The momentum inside the barrier is related to the incident momentum by ki = (co/ci)ko.
